You are an OS engineer comparing how `jj` handles branches compared to Git. In a provided repository at `/home/user/repo`, there is a single commit with a bookmark `main`. Your task is to: 1. Use `jj` to create a new commit on top of `main` that adds a file `git_semantics.txt` with the text 'git semantics'. Describe the commit as 'add git semantics'. 2. Create a bookmark named `git_branch` pointing to this new commit. 3. Use `jj` to create another new commit on top of `main` that adds a file `jj_semantics.txt` with the text 'jj semantics'. Describe the commit as 'add jj semantics'. 4. Create a bookmark named `jj_branch` pointing to this second new commit. 5. Export the bookmarks to git using `jj git export`. 6. Run a `jj log` command that shows only the `git_branch` and `jj_branch` commits, and save the output to `/home/user/comparison_log.txt`.

🎉 Task Completed
I've completed the tasks. Here's a summary of what I did:
1.  Created a new commit on top of `main` with the description "add git semantics" and added a file `git_semantics.txt` containing the text "git semantics".
2.  Created a bookmark named `git_branch` pointing to this commit.
3.  Created another new commit on top of `main` with the description "add jj semantics" and added a file `jj_semantics.txt` containing the text "jj semantics".
4.  Created a bookmark named `jj_branch` pointing to this commit.
5.  Exported the bookmarks to the underlying Git repository using `jj git export`.
6.  Generated a log showing only the `git_branch` and `jj_branch` commits and saved it to `/home/user/comparison_log.txt`.

The comparison log at `/home/user/comparison_log.txt` looks like this:
```
@  sqwuzoym test@example.com 2026-03-07 00:08:37 jj_branch c65fb034
│  add jj semantics
~

○  smvooorw test@example.com 2026-03-07 00:08:23 git_branch 30b602ca
│  add git semantics
~
```

You can verify the state of the repository by running `jj log` or `git branch` in `/home/user/repo`.

Compare file contents between two revisions\r\n  diffedit          Touch up the content changes in a revision with a diff editor\r\n  duplicate         Create new changes with the same content as     File operations\r\n  fix               Update files with formatting fixes or other changes\r\n  gerrit            Interact with Gerrit Code Review\r\n  git               Commands for working with log               Show revision history\r\n  metaedit          Modify the metadata of a revision without changing its content\r\n  new               Create a new, empty change and (by default) edit it Parallelize revisions by making them siblings\r\n  prev              Change the working copy revision relative to the parent revision\r\n  rebase            Move revisions to different parent(s)\r\n  redo              Redo the most recently undone operation\r\n  resolve           Resolve conflicted files with an external merge tool\r\n  restore           Restore paths from another revision\r\n  revert            Apply the reverse of the given revision(s)\r\n  root              Show the current workspace root directory (shortcut for `jj workspace root`)\r\n  show              Show commit description and changes in a revision\r\n  sign              Cryptographically sign a revision\r\n  simplify-parents  Simplify parent edges for the specified revision(s)\r\n  sparse            Manage which paths from the working-copy commit are present in the working copy\r\n  split             Split a revision in two\r\n  squash            Move changes from a revision into another revision\r\n  status            Show high-level repo status [default alias: st]\r\n  tag               Manage tags\r\n  undo              Undo the last operation\r\n  unsign            Drop a cryptographic signature\r\n  util              Infrequently used commands such as for generating shell completions\r\n  version           Display version information\r\n  workspace         Commands for working with workspaces\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\n  -V, --version\r\n          Print version\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation\r\n          ID is enough.\r\n          \r\n          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.\r\n          \r\n          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.\r\n          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier\r\n          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --at-op]\r\n\r\n      --debug\r\n          Enable debug logging\r\n\r\n      --color <WHEN>\r\n          When to colorize output\r\n          \r\n          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]\r\n\r\n      --quiet\r\n          Silence non-primary command output\r\n          \r\n          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working\r\n          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.\r\n          \r\n          Warnings and errors will still be printed.\r\n\r\n      --no-pager\r\n          Disable the pager\r\n\r\n      --config <NAME=VALUE>\r\n          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)\r\n          \r\n          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys. The value should be specified as a TOML\r\n          expression. If string value isn't enclosed by any TOML constructs (such as array\r\n          notation), quotes can be omitted.\r\n\r\n      --config-file <PATH>\r\n          Additional configuration files (can be repeated)\r\n\r\n'jj help --help' lists available keywords.
